text: The robotic excise system is utilized with two assistant ports, one 5 mm and one 12 mm. After docking, the surgeon positions the bowel and lifts the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), scoring the peritoneum below to achieve an avascular plane between the mesocolon and retroperitoneum. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is lifted along with the mesocolon and mesorectum, and the peritoneum is incised to identify the avascular plane above the hypogastric nerves. The IMA is traced superiorly to its root, preserving the left colic artery, and ligated beyond it, allowing medial to lateral mobilization of the mesocolon. Attention is then turned to the pelvis, where the posterior avascular plane of the total mesorectal excision (TME) is followed to mobilize the rectum posteriorly to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon focuses on the anterior lateral aspect of the total mesorectal excision (TME). The ureter is carefully lateralized and protected, while the vas deferens is identified, clipped, and divided. Small vessels in the area are cauterized using bipolar electrocautery. The peritoneal incision is extended anterolaterally, connecting the posterior and lateral planes of the TME. An anterior plane is created between the bladder and seminal vesicles, with small vessels cauterized as needed.

The surgeon continues the dissection from the left side to the anterior and right side, maintaining the bladder above and the seminal vesicles below. The right vas deferens is clipped and divided, and the avascular plane between the seminal vesicle and bladder is followed until reaching the base of the prostate and caudally to both ejaculatory ducts.

The assistant retracts the bladder anteriorly while the third arm of the robot retracts the seminal vesicle, creating space between these structures. Care is taken to avoid entering the bladder. On the right side, the posterior and lateral TME planes are connected. Anteriorly, a gauze piece is placed for identification when approaching from above. The rectum is divided to create an end-stoma, and the space of Retzius is entered to mobilize the bladder.

The surgeon identifies and divides the bilateral puboprostatic ligaments to mobilize the bladder with the prostate. The bladder is divided at its neck, avoiding entry above the trigone to prevent ureteric injury. The urethral bladder neck is opened, and the Foley catheter is withdrawn, completing the transection at the bladder neck. A previously placed gauze piece is visible, confirming the correct surgical plane.
The robotic system, utilizing its magnification capabilities, facilitates precise dissection deep into the pelvic region.

The surgeon identifies and divides the bilateral neurovascular bundles of the prostate using bipolar cautery, facilitating the complete mobilization of the specimen from the bladder. The bladder neck is then closed with Vicryl-II zero sutures in a continuous two-layer technique.

A purse string suture is placed at the site of the suprapubic catheter near the dome of the bladder, and a hole is made into the bladder to facilitate catheter entry. The Foley catheter is fixed to the anterior abdominal wall with another purse string suture at the bladder.
